coredata.dc:title: PVDF membrane immobilized acetylcholinesterase-based fluorescence biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jia
Li, Lin
Chen, Xin-Yue
Chen, Juan
coredata.dc:description: Trace residues of chlorpyrifos (organophosphorus pesticides) pose significant environmental hazards due to their acute insecticidal toxicity. Chlorpyrifos inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which heightens the risks of neurological disorders. The implications of these interactions underscore the urgent need for detection strategies to mitigate the risks associated with chlorpyrifos exposure. Considering the inherently fragile nature of AChE, a novel composite membrane (PVDF/PDA/PEI) was introduced to immobilize AChE for its excellent simplicity, portability and ease of separation. And a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor based on photoluminescent carbon dots emitting at 468 nm and cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets emitting at 568 nm were constructed. The resulting fluorescence ratio at F468/F568 was served as a ratiometric fluorescence signal for AChE assay and chlorpyrifos detection, with a detection limit of 0.103 ng/mL. This study facilitates the precise monitoring of chlorpyrifos levels in real samples, providing a reliable method to detect even trace amounts of this widely used chlorpyrifos. By offering a balance between the activity and stability of fragile AChE, facilitating the utilization of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE in the fabrication of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the purpose of chlorpyrifos detection. This advancement not only improves detection sensitivity but also contributes significantly to environmental safety and public health monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized acetylcholinesterase
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Organophosphorus pesticides
Chlorpyrifos detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Responsiveness of the CDs/CoOOH/OPD to PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
Performance evaluation of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
Chlorpyrifos detection using a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the dual-emission fluorescence sensing platform for sensitive detection of AChE and chlorpyrifos, (B) The synthetic procedure of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE.

SEM images of (A) PVDF, (B) PVDF/PDA, (C) PVDF/PDA/PEI and magnified SEM images of (D) PVDF, (E) PVDF/PDA, (F) PVDF/PDA/PEI, (G) XPS spectrum of three materials and (H) AFM morphology of PVDF, PVDF/PDA, PVDF/PDA/PEI.

(A) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of CDs and UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets (4 mg/mL CoOOH nanosheets), (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CoOOH and a mixed solution of CDs and CoOOH, (C) The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and the CDs/CoOOH, (D) Stern-Volmer plot of CDs solution after the addition of various concentrations of CoOOH.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs/CoOOH + OPD system by incubation with various concentrations of AChE, (B) The corresponding calibration graphs for the CDs/CoOOH + OPD sensing system.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of the various concentrations chlorpyrifos using a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, (B) Relationship between F468/F568 and logarithm of chlorpyrifos.
The comparison of different procedures for the assay of AChE.
Comparison of performance of various organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) detecting strategy.

row.entry: Colorimetric sensing platform based on AChE@Zn-MOF-74
PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE based ratiometric fluorescence biosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of chlorpyrifos in real samples.

head.ce:title: PVDF membrane immobilized acetylcholinesterase-based fluorescence biosensor for chlorpyrifos detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Experimental & Training Teaching Centers, Gansu University of Chinese Medicine, Lanzhou 730000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Experimental & Training Teaching Centers, Gansu University of Chinese Medicine, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Trace residues of chlorpyrifos (organophosphorus pesticides) pose significant environmental hazards due to their acute insecticidal toxicity. Chlorpyrifos inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE), which heightens the risks of neurological disorders. The implications of these interactions underscore the urgent need for detection strategies to mitigate the risks associated with chlorpyrifos exposure. Considering the inherently fragile nature of AChE, a novel composite membrane (PVDF/PDA/PEI) was introduced to immobilize AChE for its excellent simplicity, portability and ease of separation. And a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor based on photoluminescent carbon dots emitting at 468 nm and cobalt oxyhydroxide nanosheets emitting at 568 nm were constructed. The resulting fluorescence ratio at F468/F568 was served as a ratiometric fluorescence signal for AChE assay and chlorpyrifos detection, with a detection limit of 0.103 ng/mL. This study facilitates the precise monitoring of chlorpyrifos levels in real samples, providing a reliable method to detect even trace amounts of this widely used chlorpyrifos. By offering a balance between the activity and stability of fragile AChE, facilitating the utilization of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE in the fabrication of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the purpose of chlorpyrifos detection. This advancement not only improves detection sensitivity but also contributes significantly to environmental safety and public health monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorous pesticides (OPs) are the most widely used toxic and efficient agricultural insecticides, significantly enhancing the productivity and quality of agricultural products by protecting crops from pests, weeds, and diseases [1]. Chlorpyrifos (O, O-diethyl-O-(3,5,6-trichloro-2-pyridinyl) phosphorothioate), one of the most commonly used OPs in agriculture, is classified by the World Health Organisation (WHO) as a Class II pesticide, indicating medium toxicity [2]. The acute insecticidal toxicity of chlorpyrifos arises from trace residues that harm the environment and inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE). This inhibition is strongly associated with the binding of AChE and the irreversible inhibition of its activity, leading to the accumulation of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ATCh), which increases the risk of Alzheimers disease, Parkinsons disease, and other neurological disorders [3,4]. Despite their benefits for agricultural development, the extensive use and high exposure to chlorpyrifos pose significant threats to human health and the environment [5]. In this regard, it is imperative to develop highly sensitive, efficient, and convenient methods for detecting and determining chlorpyrifos in real samples to ensure human health and food safety.

Numerous conventional analytical methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography [6], gas chromatography/mass spectrometry [7], electrochemical analysis [8], and colorimetric assay [9], have been utilized to detect OPs residues. For example, Ezhil Vilian et al. developed a straightforward method to synthesize AuNC-MoSe2-PC coated glassy carbon electrodes, which served as highly efficient electrocatalyst for the selective and ultrafast detection of chlorpyrifos-pesticide [10]. Similarly, Yan et al. utilized a γ-Fe2O3/thiophene-sulfur-doped graphene magnetic nanocomposite as an artificial nanozyme, enabling rapid and precise chlorpyrifos detection [11]. Meanwhile, Hui et al. introduced graphitic carbon nitride/bismuth ferrite nanocomposites as a single peroxidase-like probe for the first time, creating a dual-readout immunochromatographic assay designed for the multiplexed detection of pesticide residues [12]. Although these methods have been successfully applied, they possess several drawbacks, such as being time-consuming, requiring expensive instrumentation, and needing skilled technicians, which limit their widespread application. Fortunately, fluorescent biosensors have emerged as a promising strategy for rapidly and sensitively monitoring and detecting OPs, opening up the possibility of designing fluorescent sensing platforms for OPs detection [13].

For instance, Li et al. designed a robust portable kit based on gold nanoclusters/MnO2 composites-based hydrogels, featuring a ratiometric fluorescent signal for precise monitoring of paraoxon residues and degradation in Chinese cabbages [14]. Similarly, Chang et al. developed a straightforward paper-based fluorescent sensor, which proved to be a powerful tool for the rapid, naked-eye detection of AChE activity and OPs [15]. Additionally, Cai et al. fabricated AuNCs@ZIF-8 with strong fluorescence properties, employing it for real-time monitoring of pesticide contamination [16]. These biosensors not only exhibit unique luminescence characteristics, but also effectively minimize background signals, thereby enhancing sensitivity for OPs detection. However, the catalytic reactions in these fluorescent biosensors faces limitations, particularly due to the fragile nature of AChE activity and the challenges in controlling enzymatic reactions. To overcome this issue, our approach focuses on fabricating a robust support to immobilize AChE, significantly improving its stability and practical applicability.

Currently, various fluorescent biosensors, such as metallic nanoclusters [17], semiconductor quantum dots [18], carbon dots [19], and organic materials [20], have been adopted as signaling probes to detect OPs using off–on or ratiometric strategies. Among them, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn extensive attention due to their outstanding properties, including low toxicity, mass synthesis capability, simple preparation, excellent fluorescence performance, environmental friendliness, and good water solubility [21].

Due to the intrinsically fragile nature of AChE makes it vulnerable to external environmental interference, which can expose its active sites or disrupt its conformation, significantly limiting its use. To address this, a practical and convenient enzyme immobilization method, which physically or chemically confines the enzyme onto or within a support, was employed to achieve a more active, robust, and reusable immobilized enzyme [22]. Positively charged polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) microfiltration membranes, known for their ease of separation, portability, and environmental protection, were chosen as the support. Poly (ethylene imine) (PEI), a branched polymer with numerous amine groups, was used for grafting with dopamine (DA) to create a transition layer [23,24]. PEI molecules were covalently grafted onto the PVDF surface via Michael addition and Schiff base reactions with the quinone functional groups of poly(dopamine) (PDA), forming an active layer. The cross-linked PDA-PEI layer enhanced the hydrophilicity, structural stability, and compatibility of the PVDF membrane surface. Subsequently, the PVDF/PDA/PEI composite was constructed to immobilize AChE, resulting in a system with desirable properties of simplicity, portability, and ease of separation, facilitating subsequent analysis.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was designed for the sensitive detecting AChE and chlorpyrifos by combining the intrinsic properties of CoOOH nanosheets and CDs. The utilization of highly photoluminescent CDs were green-synthesized from spinach using a one-step synthesis strategy. And Cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH), a type of two-dimensional nanomaterial, was utilized as a fluorescence sensor due to its peroxidase-like activity and fluorescence quenching capability [25,26]. As illustrated in Fig. 1 A, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by CoOOH nanosheets due to the overlap between the absorption spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets and the emission spectrum of CDs at 468 nm. In route ①, upon the addition of chlorpyrifos into the system, the PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE acivity was broken, enzymatic reaction can not proceed and no thiocholine (TCh) was generated. Subsequently, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was catalytically oxidized into 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD) by CoOOH nanosheets, resulting in a new fluorescence emission at 568 nm. In route ②, the presence of TCh, produced by the enzymatic reaction between ATCh and PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE, could decompose the CoOOH nanosheets to Co2+, triggering the recovery of CDs fluorescence at 468 nm and weakening the oxidation of OPD. Thus, the fluorescence ratio at F468/F568 serves as the ratiometric fluorescence signal for the detection of AChE and chlorpyrifos.
Detailed information about all the materials and apparatus used in the experiments can be found in the Supplementary Material.

The immobilization of AChE on PVDF/PDA/PEI involved the following steps, as shown in Fig. 1B:
(1)
Preparation of dopamine solution: An aqueous dopamine solution was prepared by dissolving dopamine hydrochloride (2.0 mg/mL) in Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 10.0 mM).
(2)
Formation of PDA layer (Fig. 1B, Ⅰ): The PVDF membrane was rinsed with deionized water several times, immersed in the dopamine solution, and shaken (100 rpm) at room temperature for 4.0 h. The oxidization of dopamine to polydopamine (PDA) involves the participation of oxygen, so this step was conducted with the solution exposed to air [23,24]. Afterwards, the membrane was removed from the solution, thoroughly washed with deionized water, resulting in a firm PDA layer deposited on the PVDF membrane. The resultant PVDF/PDA membrane was obtained for further functionalization.
(3)
PEI coating (Fig. 1B, Ⅱ): The PVDF/PDA membrane was soaked in an aqueous PEI solution (2.0 mg/mL, 20 mL) and shaken (120 rpm) at 60 °C for 8.0 h. The resultant membrane, defined as PVDF/PDA/PEI membrane, was then removed, rinsed thoroughly with deionized water, and stored in deionized water for further use.
(4)
AChE immobilization (Fig. 1B, Ⅲ): The PVDF/PDA/PEI membrane was immersed in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 6.5, 10 mM) containing AChE at a concentration of 1.0 U/mL and shaken (120 rpm) at 25 °C for 1 h. After immobilization, the membrane was removed and washed with deionized water to remove any unfixed enzyme.
The resulting PVDF/PDA/PEI membrane with immobilized AChE was ready for use in further experiments.

Briefly, washed spinach (5 g) was ground into a slurry and added to deionized water (20 mL), then mixed with 10 mL of ethanediamine via ultrasound. The suspension was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 165 °C for 6 h. The resultant dark brown mixture was diluted with 20 mL of deionized water and then centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove larger particles. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane for 48 h. The prepared blue fluorescent N-doped CDs were then diluted with water and stored at 4 °C for use.

The fabrication of CoOOH was based on previous literature with minor revisions [27]. An NaOH aqueous solution (250 μL, 1.0 M) was added to a CoCl2 aqueous solution (1 mL, 10.0 mM) and sonicated for 1 min. Subsequently, NaClO·5H2O (50 μL, 0.9 M) was added dropwise to the mixture and sonicated for 20 min. The CoOOH nanosheets were then collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min), washed with water, and dried.

A piece of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE (diameter = 3 mm) was incubated with 25 μL of ATChI (50 mM) for 30 min at 37 °C. After incubation, the PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE was removed from the reaction system to terminate the reaction. Subsequently, 25 μL of CDs and 100 μL of CoOOH nanosheets (4 mg/mL) were added to the above mixture. Then, 100 μL of OPD (70 mM) and sodium acetate buffer (pH = 4.5, 100 mM) were introduced into the solution, and the reaction was maintained for 20 min at 50 °C. The mixture was diluted to 1000 μL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4), and fluorescence spectra were collected with an excitation wavelength of 385 nm.

Various concentrations of chlorpyrifos (25 μL) were incubated with a piece of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE (diameter = 3 mm) for 15 min, followed by incubating with ATChI (25 μL) for 30 min at 37°C. The PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE was then removed from the reaction system to terminate the reaction. CDs (25 μL) was introduced into the system, and OPD (70 mM) in sodium acetate buffer (pH = 4.5, 100 mM) was further catalyzed by CoOOH nanosheets (100 μL) for another 20 min at 50 °C. The mixture was diluted to 1000 μL with Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4), and fluorescence spectra were collected with an excitation wavelength of 385 nm.

Real samples, including tap water, Yellow River water, and milk, were used to investigate the practical applications of the fluorescent biosensors. Tap water (5.0 mL) was pre-filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The collected Yellow River water (5.0 mL) was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain the Yellow River water sample. Milk was diluted 25 times without additional treatments. These samples were then spiked with 2.5, 5 and 15 ng/mL chlorpyrifos. The prepared samples were analyzed using the aforementioned procedures.

The surface morphology of the original PVDF membrane and the modified PVDF membranes was visualized using high-resolution SEM, and the micrographs are shown in Fig. 2 . The original PVDF membrane exhibited a crosslinked state with a clear surface (Fig. 2A, Fig. 2D). In contrast, the PVDF/PDA composite membrane surface appeared rugged with numerous nanostructured protrusions, as depicted in Fig. 2B. The formation of PDA nanoaggregates on the membrane surface is evident (Fig. 2E), resulting from both physical aggregation and covalent crosslinking. The inhomogeneous distribution of PDA nanostructured protrusions on the membrane surface may be attributed to the conjugation and accumulation of PDA nanoaggregates. Following PEI grafting, the membrane surface became relatively smooth, with only a few small protrusions observable, as shown in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2F. The addition of PEI promoted the reaction of amine groups of the PEI molecules with the quinone functional groups on the PDA layer surface through Michael addition reaction and/or Schiff base reaction, which reflected the reduction of protrusions on the membrane surface [28]. It was probably due to grafting of PEI molecules, whose chains were rather flexible, blocking the pores of the PDA layer. The appearance of amine groups and the decrease of roughness might be beneficial to the immobilization of AChE and the mass transfer.

To further investigate the chemical composition of the PVDF membrane and its modifications, XPS analysis was employed, and the results are shown in Fig. 2G. The PVDF membrane exhibited characteristic peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, and F 1 s. In the spectrum of the PVDF/PDA composite membrane, a distinct N 1 s peak appeared, attributed to the presence of modified PDA. Additionally, the atomic percentage of F in the PVDF/PDA composite membrane declined compared to the PVDF membrane, indicating the formation of a thicker PDA layer. Following PEI grafting, the atomic percentage of N further increased in the PVDF/PDA/PEI membrane, confirming successful PEI anchoring and an increased density of amine groups on the membrane surface.

AFM analysis of the membranes was conducted to observe three-dimensional images and measure membrane roughness, as shown in Fig. 2H. The roughness average (Ra) of the original PVDF membrane was 119 nm, which significantly increased to 205 nm after PDA modification, likely due to the deposition of PDA nanoaggregates. After PEI grafting, the Ra decreased to 136 nm. These changes in membrane roughness correlated closely with the observed morphological transformations, validating that the deposition of PDA and grafting of PEI substantially altered the membrane surface. The changes of membrane performance, including surface roughness, surface functional groups and surface charge property may have a positive effect on subsequent enzyme immobilization.

Further investigation using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CoOOH nanosheets revealed characteristic absorption peaks (Fig. S1A). The absorption band at approximately 3421 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the –OH group. Peaks observed at 1636 cm−1 and 580 cm−1 are attributed to the Co-O double bond and Co-O2– vibrations within the framework of CoOOH nanosheets [27,29].

TEM images (Fig. S1B) revealed hexagonal morphology structures with an estimated flake size of approximately 62.8 nm (inset in Fig. S1B) for CoOOH nanosheets. Dynamic light scattering indicated a hydrodynamic size of about 88.2 nm (Fig. S1C), which is larger than the size distribution observed in TEM. This discrepancy suggests the aggregation of CoOOH nanosheets in the solvent system. Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of CoOOH nanosheets (Fig. S1D) further confirmed the presence of Co and O elements, consistent with previously reports [30].
The morphology of CDs was characterized by TEM, and the images revealed well-dispersed spherical morphology of CDs with a size diameter of approximately 1.3 nm (Fig. S2).

The fluorescence stability of carbon quantum dots (CDs) is of paramount importance, serving as a foundational assurance for the detection of AChE and chlorpyrifos. In this study, we meticulously evaluated the effects of several factors, NaCl concentration, UV irradiation time, pH, and temperature on the fluorescence stability of CDs, with results summarized in Fig. S3.

As illustrated in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence of CDs demonstrated remarkable stability across a range of NaCl concentrations, highlighting their impressive salt tolerance. Similarly, we assessed the stability of fluorescence intensity under various durations of UV exposure (Fig. S3B). Remarkably, even with prolonged UV irradiation, the fluorescence of CDs remained largely unchanged. Turning our attention to pH levels, Fig. S3C reveals significant fluctuations in fluorescence intensity under acidic (pH < 5) and alkaline (pH > 7) conditions, which might due to the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs in acidic and basic media [31]. The fluorescence properties of CDs are affected by environmental factors, and the pH value is an important factor. Under acidic conditions (pH < 5), the surface functional groups of CDs may undergo protonation or charge transfer reactions, resulting in fluorescence quenching of CDs. While under alkaline conditions (pH > 7), the quenching effect occurs, resulting in the decrease of fluorescence of CDs [27].

Finally, we explored the influence of temperature on the stability of CDs, as shown in Fig. S3D. An increase in temperature resulted in a slight decline in fluorescence intensity. This observation can be attributed to the nature of fluorescence, which involves radiative transitions. As temperature rises, the enhanced molecular motion and the increased rate of non-radiative transitions lead to a relative weakening of the radiative transition rate, ultimately resulting in diminished fluorescence intensity. In summary, our thorough analysis indicates that the synthesized CDs exhibit commendable stability, reinforcing their potential for reliable applications in detection.

To explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs induced by CoOOH, the fluorescence emission of the CDs in conjunction with the UV–vis absorption spectra of CoOOH nanosheets were assessed. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CoOOH, depicted in Fig. 3 A, revealed a broad absorption range from 350 nm to 600 nm, significantly overlapping with the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs. This notable overlap suggests that CoOOH nanosheets may have a substantial quenching effect on the CDs.

To gain deeper insights into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis spectra of CDs, CoOOH, and a mixed solution of CDs and CoOOH were collected. As shown in Fig. 3B, the absorption peak around 280 nm exhibited a slight blue shift upon the addition of CoOOH. This shift may indicate the formation of a complex between the two materials, leading us to speculate that the most probable reaction mechanism is static quenching.

In addition to this analysis, we determined the fluorescence lifetimes of both the CDs and the CDs/CoOOH mixture. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the calculated fluorescence lifetimes were 6.33 ns for the CDs and 6.26 ns for the mixture, suggesting that fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the CDs and CoOOH is absent [32].

To validate our findings, we applied the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation (Eq. (1)) to further analyze the quenching mechanism. The equation is expressed as follows [33]:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C = 1 +
K q
τ 0
C
Here, F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of CoOOH, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime without CoOOH is denoted as
τ 0
, while C indicates the concentration of CoOOH.
K
SV
and
K q
are the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and the bimolecular quenching constant, respectively.

As illustrated in Fig. 3D, a strong linear correlation between F0/F and concentration of CoOOH was observed (F0/F = 0.9810 + 0.0139CCoOOH).
K
SV
value was calculated to be 1.39 × 104 M−1, while
K q
value was determined to be 2.20 × 1012 M−1·s−1. Given that
K q
exceeds 1.0 × 1010 M−1·s−1 [34], this indicates that the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is primarily due to static quenching rather than dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responsiveness of the CDs/CoOOH/OPD to PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
ce:section.ce:para: CDs was served as the fluorescence probe to facilitate enzyme sensing. The excitation-dependent emission characteristics of CDs were investigated by varying the excitation wavelength from 380 nm to 430 nm. Fig. S4A showed that the emission intensity reached its maximum at an excitation wavelength of 385 nm, and the fluorescence emission spectrum exhibited a significant red shift with increasing excitation wavelength. This phenomenon, a behavior excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence, is commonly observed in carbon quantum dots and may result from variations in surface functional groups and the maldistribution of the particle size of CDs. The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF ) of the CDs was measured to be 22.2 %.

Based on the optimal excitation wavelengths for CDs and oxOPD, strong fluorescence at 385 nm in their excitation spectra was chosen for subsequent experiments (Fig. S4B). In this study, CoOOH nanosheets functioned as a bifunctional nanomaterial, served both as a quencher to attenuate the fluorescence of CDs and as a peroxidase-like nanozyme to oxidize OPD, generating the fluorescent product oxOPD.

As depicted in Fig. S4C, CDs exhibited prominent fluorescence emission at 468 nm, which was dramatically quenched by CoOOH nanosheets. Moreover, CoOOH nanosheets catalytically oxidized non-fluorescence OPD to fluorescent oxOPD with emission at 568 nm. The successful oxidation of OPD to oxOPD upon the addition of CoOOH was confirmed, as shown in Fig. S4D. These results underscored the dual functionality of CoOOH nanosheets as both a quencher and a peroxidase-like oxidant.

Furthermore, the strong oxidizing capability of CoOOH nanosheets stems from the presence of Co3+. Research has demonstrated that CoOOH undergoes redox reactions involving reductants, resulting in the reduction of CoOOH to Co2+ ions [35–37]. Upon the introduction of AChE and ATChI, AChE hydrolyzes ATChI to produce TCh, which can decompose CoOOH nanosheets into Co2+ ions, as illustrated in Equation (2). This process leads to the restoration of the quenched fluorescence of CDs at 468 nm and the reduction in fluorescence emission of oxOPD at 568 nm.
(2).

In the experiment evaluating the responsiveness of CDs/CoOOH/OPD to PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE, the experimental parameters of CoOOH nanosheets concentration and OPD concentration were carefully scrutinized. As depicted in Fig. S5A, increasing the concentration of CoOOH nanosheets enhanced their quenching efficiency and peroxidase-like capability, resulting in the reduced fluorescence at 468 nm and enhanced fluorescence at 568 nm. Similarly, Fig. S5B demonstrates a dramatic decrease in the fluorescence ratio at F468/F568 with increasing CoOOH nanosheets concentration, stabilizing at 4 mg/mL. Identically, Fig. S5C demonstrates that increasing OPD concentration led to a substantial decrease in the fluorescence ratio at F468/F568, reaching a plateau at 70 mM OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
Performance evaluation of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the best performance of the PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE, optimizations of catalytic activity and experimental conditions were conducted, which were presented in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7. And the kinetics performance of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE was compared with free AChE, the detailed information was illustrated in Fig. S8.

A significant limitation of free AChE is its inability to be reused due to its solubility and inseparability after use. The reusability of immobilized AChE is crucial for making the process economic. In this study, after each activity measurement, the immobilized enzyme was retrieved and washed several times with Tris-HCl buffer. The activity assay was then repeated for subsequent measurements. As depicted in Fig. S9A, approximately 80 % of the initial activity of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE was retained after 7 cycles. demonstrating relatively favorable stability, likely due to the strong interaction between the substrate and enzyme.

Enzyme immobilization aims to maximize enzyme activity while maintaining enzyme stability [38]. Thus, the protective capacity of PVDF/PDA/PEI for AChE was investigated under various conditions, including exposure to organic solvent, pH variations, and different temperature environments. PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE showed reduced sensitivity to ethanol (10 %, v/v), methanol (10 %, v/v), acidic conditions (0.1 M HCl), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO, 10 %, v/v) and elevated temperatures (50 °C and 70 °C) compared to free AChE (Fig. S9B).

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the proposed platform for AChE activity was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, increasing the concentration of AChE led to the decomposition of more CoOOH nanosheets, resulting in a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity at 468 nm and a decrease at 568 nm. The inset of Fig. 4A illustrates that the fluorescence color shifted from orange to blue as the AChE concentration increased. Consequently, the fluorescence ratio at F468/F568 was used as the signal output for AChE analysis within the concentration range of 5 × 10-3 to 2.5 U/mL. This exhibited a linear relationship, given by the equation F468/F568 = 0.262CAChE + 0.674, with a corresponding regression coefffcient of 0.999 (Fig. 4B). Compared with previous reported methods [38,39], the CDs/CoOOH + OPD system biosensor not only demonstrates operational simplicity and low toxicity, but also employs peroxisase-like active materials to catalyze the oxidation of substrates, generating fluorescent products. This results in an accurate and sensitive detection of AChE. The analytical performance of the present ratiometric fluorescence sensing system for AChE assay was compared with various previously reported protocols [40,41], as shown in Table 1 . It is evident that the present protocol provides superior assay sensitivity among the compared approaches.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chlorpyrifos detection using a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: Using huperzine A, an AChE inhibitor, as a model, the detection of AChE inhibitors was examined. As shown in Fig. S10, the inhibition rate increased with higher doses of huperzine A added to the fluorescence sensing platform. A range of huperzine A concentrations (0.15 nM-22.5 μM) were tested to construct a regression equation. The inhibition rate (I%) was used to quantify the enzyme’s inhibitory capacity, calculated by the formula I(%)=(FE-FI)/(FE-F0), where F0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity without AChE, FE is the fluorescence intensity after recovery by AChE without the inhibitor, and FI is the fluorescence intensity after the inhibitory effect of the inhibitor. Using this formula, the IC50 of huperzine A was determined to be 12.99 μM. This result robustly demonstrates that the established sensing platform is capable of evaluating the inhibitory potential of AChE inhibitors.

Inspired by the robust biocatalytic capacity and stability of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE, a reliable enzyme-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was developed for the sensitive detection of organophosphorus pesticides. Chlorpyrifos, a representative organophosphorus pesticide, was chosen as a model to explore the application of the fabricated method.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is an esterase that hydrolyzes ester-containing compounds. Its active site contains three key amino acids: histidine, serine, and aspartate. The hydroxyl group of the serine residue strongly interacts with the P=O bond in chlorpyrifos, forming phosphorylated enzyme complexes, which result in food contamination and pesticide poisoning [2,47]. Therefore, sensitive monitoring of chlorpyrifos is of great importance.

The fluorescence sensing principle relies on the inhibition of AChE activity by chlorpyrifos, which prevents the enzyme from catalyzing the hydrolysis of ATChI to produce thiocholine (TCh). The quantitative detection of chlorpyrifos is based on change in the fluorescence intensity ratio at F468/F568. By monitoring these changes, the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform enables sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos. The quantitative assay was conducted by introducing standard chlorpyrifos solutions ranging from 4.0 ng/mL to 1000 ng/mL. As the chlorpyrifos concentration increased, the fluorescence response of CDs increased while the fluorescence response of oxOPD diminished, leading to a notable change in the fluorescence intensity ratio at F468/F568 (Fig. 5 A). The results showed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F468/F568) and the logarithm of chlorpyrifos concentrations (Fig. 5B). The calibration data were linearly fitted as F468/F568 = 0.518Log[Chlorpyrifos] + 0.940, demonstrating the efficient energy transfer effect between CDs and CoOOH nanosheets. The detection limit (LOD) and quantification limit (LOQ) for the chlorpyrifos biosensor were determined using 3σ/S and 10σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample calculated from 10 parallel blank tests, and S is the slope of the curve. The LOD and LOQ were calculated to be 0.103 ng/mL and 0.34 ng/mL, respectively. Compared to reported chlorpyrifos biosensors, the present biosensor exhibited a lower detection limit and a wider linearity range, as clearly summarized in Table 2 .

The capacity of the sensing platform to selectively detect organophosphorus pesticides is vital for evaluating its practicality. Notably, other organophosphorus pesticides, such as profenofos, acetamiprid, glyphosate, dimethoate, and phoxim, possess a similar phosphate skeleton structure. This structural similarity leads to the inhibition of AChE and triggers a marked ratiometric fluorescence response, as illustrated in Fig. S11A. Such findings underscore the platform's extensive applicability across a range of organophosphorus pesticides, enhancing its potential for effective detection.

The anti-interference capacity of the ratiometric fluorescence probe was investigated by introducing various coexisting species typically encountered in biological samples, including metal ions, proteins, and pesticide (Na+, K+, urea, Glu, Trp, and Chlorpyrifos). As shown in Fig. S11B, the fluorescence intensity ratio at F468/F568 presented a pronounced response towards chlorpyrifos, while exhibiting relatively weak changes in the presence of interfering substances that do not inhibit AChE activity. These results emphasize the robust specificity and anti-interference capabilities of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform. This resilience can likely be attributed to its inherent correction mechanism, which effectively minimizes background interference in the dual-emission fluorescence sensor, ensuring reliable detection even in complex biological environments.

To further validate the applicability of the proposed platform for sensing chlorpyrifos in real samples, its performance was tested by monitoring chlorpyrifos in tap water, Yellow River water, and milk. The recovery of chlorpyrifos was measured using the standard addition method at concentrations of 0, 2.5, 5 and 15 ng/mL. As shown in Table 3 , the spiking recoveries of chlorpyrifos ranged from 98.47 % to 103.1 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) from 0.88 % to 11.7 % for these real samples. These results indicate no significant interference from the sample matrices and demonstrate relatively high reproducibility. The outcomes are consistent with those previously reported.

In summary, this work introduces a novel strategy to balance the activity and stability of fragile AChE, enabling the effective use of PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE in the fabrication of a CDs/CoOOH ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the purpose of chlorpyrifos detection. This sensor achieves an impressively low detection limit of 0.103 ng/mL. By employing PVDF/PDA/PEI immobilized AChE as a stable catalyst to initiate the reaction, the method leverages a ratiometric fluorescent biosensor to detect chlorpyrifos with high precision. This approach not only improves the stability of the otherwise fragile free enzyme, but also simplifies reaction control, facilitating accurate and reliable chlorpyrifos detection. Beyond improving the practicality of AChE, this innovative technique paves the way for advanced applications in sensitive environmental monitoring and pesticide residue analysis.

Jia Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lin Li: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Validation. Xin-Yue Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Juan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2024-pd02), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22404069), Project of the State Drug Administration-Key Laboratory of Quality Control of Chinese Medicinal Materials and Decoction Pieces, China (No. 2024GSMPA-KL06), University Teacher Innovation Fund Project, Department of Education of Gansu Province, China (No. 2023B-103) and Chinese “Double First-class” construction funds (No. 561120206).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113197.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Double-sided electrohydrodynamic jet printing of two-dimensional electrode array in paper-based digital microfluidics. Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 2019, 282, 831-837.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-site chip-based colorimetric quantitation of organophosphorus pesticides using an office scanner
